title: Neuropsychologic Findings in Friedreich's Ataxia
abstract:
Neuropsychologic Findings in Friedreich's Ataxia
Rose A. Fehrenbach; Claus-W. Wallesch, MD; Detlef Claus, MD
\s=b\Fifteen
patients
with
Friedreich's
ataxia underwent a battery of neuropsy-
chologic tests. All subjects had normal
visual acuity. There was no indication that
Friedreich's ataxia is
a dementing dis-
ease. The patients showed an impairment
in tests of spatial construction and in one
test of conceptual perception. Cerebellar
structures may be involved in certain spa-
tial tasks, and a lack of training of action
in space, as a result of the disease, may
also account for the deficits. The motor
and somatosensory impairment could be
ruled out as a major cause.
(Arch Neurol 1984;41:306-308)
"pMedreich's ataxia is
a heredode-
generative disease with onset in
childhood or adolescence. In the ner¬
vous system, the disease primarily
affects the posterior columns of the
spinal cord, the pyramidal tracts, the
spinocerebellar tracts, and the cere¬
bellum. Cortical atrophy accentuated
in the frontal and parietal lobes has
also been described.1·2 In one report,
17% of the patients with Friedreich's
ataxia were reported to show cortical
atrophy
on
computed
tomography
(CT).3
Since the first comment
on that
topic by Bell and Carmichael,4 it has
been
debated whether
Friedreich's
ataxia
is
a
dementing
disease,5·6
whether there is a subpopulation with
intellectual deterioration,4·7 whether
the disease leads to specific, eg, non¬
verbal, intellectual impairments,8·9 or
whether patients with Friedreich's
ataxia
are
intellectually
normal
except for effects of their physical
handicap or psychiatric disorders.10-12
PATIENTS AND METHODS
Patients
We examined 15 patients with Fried¬
reich's ataxia diagnosed on the basis of
the criteria of Andermann et al,13 Bar¬
beau,14 Becker,15 Bodechtel and Schrader,16
and Refsum and Skre.17 They were part of a
larger series, whose CT findings and visu¬
ally evoked potentials (VEPs)
were
re¬
ported previously.3'8
The patients were descendants of eight
families. Thirteen had at least one affected
relative. There were 12 male and three
female patients, and the mean age was 42.3
years (SD, 11.6 years). The mean duration
of illness was 28.4 years (SD, 10.8 years).
All patients exhibited pyramidal, cerebel¬
lar, and funicular sensory symptoms and
either scoliosis or foot deformities. Two
patients could still walk freely; nine pri¬
marily used a wheelchair. Eight were still
fully employed; two of these worked in a
sheltered workshop. Subjects with a cor¬
rected visual acuity of less than 5/10 in one
eye or oculomotor disturbances that af¬
fected smooth reading of small print were
excluded,
as
were patients with
severe
motor
or articulatory disturbances that
precluded
neuropsychologic
assessment.
This selection for rather benign courses
might partly explain the relatively high
average age of the patients included in our
study. Furthermore, patients younger than
18 years were excluded because the tests
used in the study cannot be applied to that
age group. Six patients had VEPs within
the normal range, five showed abnormal
latencies or interocular differences, and in
four no VEP study had been performed.
Fifteen normal subjects (patients with
peripheral neurologic or surgical disease,
visitors to the hospital, or nonacademic
staff), who were matched with the patients
with Friedreich's ataxia for age, sex, and
level of education, served as controls.
Method
Testing was performed in
one to two
sessions in the Neuropsychological Labo¬
ratory of the Department of Neurology,
Ulm University, Ulm, West Germany, or in
a quiet room at the patient's home. Assess¬
ment
was discontinued if
any sign of
fatigue occurred.
Digit Span, Arithmetic, Similarities, Vocab¬
ulary,
Picture
Arrangement,
and
Block
Design.—According to Wechsler et al," the
tests in the German version of the Wechs¬
ler Adult Intelligence Scale (HAWIE) in
combination correlate highly with Wechs-
ler's construct of intelligence. The perfor¬
mance in the subtests, however, depends on
short-term memory (digit span), mathe¬
matical abilities (arithmetic), abstraction,
categorization and linguistic abilities (sim¬
ilarities), educational level and linguistic
abilities (vocabulary), conceptual percep¬
tion (picture arrangement), and visuocon-
structive abilities (block design). For the
picture arrangement and the block design
tests, two scorings, with and without speed
credits, were done to take into account the
motor deficits of patients with Friedreich's
ataxia.
Standard Progressive Matrices.—This test
was constructed by Raven20 to
assess
a
factor of intelligence independent from
language. The subject has to indicate the
missing part of a matrix of geometrical
designs in a multiple-choice array.
Wisconsin Card Sorting Test.—In this test,
the subject is required to match cardboard
squares,
on which colored symbols
are
printed, with four target cards according
to shifting criteria of color, form, and
number.21 The task is constructed to mea¬
sure abilities of abstraction, categoriza¬
tion, and development and shift of behav¬
ioral strategies, as well as a tendency to
perseverate. A simplified evaluation was
used for this test, in which only the num¬
ber of correct responses was considered;
124 stimulus cards were given in random
order. If the subject achieved six sorting
criteria with a smaller number of cards,
the test was discontinued and the number
of correct responses was extrapolated.
Three-Dimensional
Mental
Folding.—
Three-dimensional mental folding,22 a test
of visuoconstructive abilities, is similar to
the three-dimensional
space test of the
General Aptitude Test Battery.23 A two-
dimensional drawing of the unfolded sides
of an object is presented. The subject is
asked
to
choose
the
three-dimensional
solution from four drawings of geometrical
objects in perspective views. The test lasts
six minutes and contains 40 items. The
scores are the number of items attempted
and the number solved correctly. In this
task
no
movement by
the
patient
is
required.
Accepted for publication June 11, 1983.
From the Department of Neurology and the
Language Pathology Unit, Ulm University, Ulm,
West Germany. Dr Wallesch is now with Frei-
burg (West Germany) University; Dr Claus is
with Medizinische Hochschule, L\l=u"\beck,West
Germany.
Reprint requests to Department of Clinical
Neurology and Neurophysiology, Freiburg Uni-
versity,
Hansastr
9,
D-7800 Freiburg,
West
Germany (Dr Wallesch).
Downloaded From: http://archneur.jamanetwork.com/ by a Oakland University User  on 05/31/2015
 Sequential Concept Formation.—Sequen¬
tial concept formation is a task of learning
and retention within the range of minutes
that can be solved by verbal and nonverbal
strategies. The subjects
were asked to
learn
and finally predict correctly the
sequence of colors of
an ordinary deck
of playing cards sorted in the color
se¬
quence red-red-black-black-red-black-r-r-
b-b-r-b-_They were told that the cards
had been arranged in a certain, repetitive
sequence of colors, that they had to guess
the color of the next card in the beginning,
and learn the sequence simultaneously to
be able to predict from memory. The score
was the number of correct predictions in
the first 72 trials. Thirty-six correct, of
course, corresponded to chance level.
Test Battery
The construction of the battery followed
two objectives. First, since we intended to
cover an inventory of higher mental func¬
tions, the battery resembled an extended
version of routine neuropsychologic assess¬
ment. A more specific approach was not to
be considered adequate, since the findings
previously reported for patients with Fried¬
reich's ataxia hardly allowed predictions.
Second,
we preferred
those
tests
that
required no or only limited movements of
the patients.
Statistical Analysis
The results were analyzed by the Mann-
Whitney U test.25 In the context of this
study, this test cannot be regarded as a test
of significance in the strict sense, because
we investigated a number of variables in a
limited number of patients. The compari¬
sons were therefore not independent. The
 values given in Table 1 are thus only
comparative measurements of the relative
size of differences.
RESULTS
Table
1 shows that the patients
with Friedreich's ataxia and the nor¬
mal
control
subjects
were
well
matched. In most tests there were
only minute differences between the
two
collectives.
Both
groups
were
comparable for intelligence, as mea¬
sured by the verbal IQ and the pro¬
gressive matrices, as well as for tasks
of
categorization
(Wisconsin
card
sorting) and learning (sequential con¬
cept formation). Major diiferences,
which reached the 1% level of signifi¬
cance,
were found in both tests of
spatial-visuoconstructive
abilities
(block design, mental folding) and in
the picture arrangement.
Whereas
mental folding test requires no move¬
ment of the subject, the patients with
Friedreich's ataxia were handicapped
by their ataxic deficit of manual dex¬
terity in the block design and picture
arrangement tests, since speed of per¬
formance is credited in both tests.
Table 2 shows the HAWIE perfor¬
mance test results without speed cred-

its. Since the time limits of Wechsler's
scale were ample enough to allow all
patients with Friedreich's ataxia to
complete their solutions in time, an
untimed condition was not necessary.
Whereas the differences in the block
design test lost significance, those in
the picture arrangement test and the
performance IQ reached the 5% level
even if speed was not credited.
The
picture
arrangement,
block
design, and mental folding tests rely
on
the
visual modality. Although
patients
whose
visual
acuity
was
insufficient had been excluded, a pro¬
portion of the patients with Fried¬
reich's
ataxia
exhibited
abnormal
VEPs. Some damage within the visual
system must be considered for these
patients. We therefore compared the
performances of five patients with
abnormal VEPs with six patients,
with
normal
VEPs.
The
results
showed no evidence of an impairment
in tests relying on the visual modality
in patients with abnormal VEPs.
The effects of disturbed eye move¬
ments on the test results cannot be
analyzed on the basis of our data. All
patients with Friedreich's ataxia but
no control subject exhibited some ocu¬
lomotor deficit.
Those patients, in
whom smooth reading of small print
was affected, had been excluded from
the study.
COMMENT
Our data give no evidence that Fried¬
reich's ataxia is a dementing disease.
The
neuropsychologic
impairments
we found are related to a defined set
of functions, whereas a wide spectrum
of other cognitive functions appears
intact. However,
as Marie26 stated:
"Chaque famille interprète .. .la mal¬
adie de Friedreich à sa façon" (Each
family interprets Friedreich's disease
in its own way). There might be
a
subgroup of a hereditary Friedreich's
ataxia-dementia
combination
that
escaped our sample. Bell and Carmi-
chael" reported mental handicaps in

group, our patients with Friedreich's
ataxia showed deficits in the picture
arrangement and block design sub-
tests of the HAWIE and in the mental
folding test. The impairment in the
block design seems partly due to the
motor handicap of patients with Fried¬
reich's ataxia, since the difference
from the normal controls is reduced if
speed credits are not given. The men¬
tal folding, however, as a test of spa¬
tial construction, requires no move¬
Our data thus support the findings
of Geoffroy et al8 and Bouchard et al9
that patients with Friedreich's ataxia
a deficit in tests of nonverbal

are impaired in tests of spatial
construction (mental folding) and in a
sequential organization (picture
These results can be interpreted in
different ways. It seems unlikely that
the deficits reflect right hemisphere
damage leaving functions of the left
hemisphere intact, although similar
deficits have been reported for lesions
in the right hemisphere.27·28 The defi¬
cits could be related to the findings of
Spiller7 of a frontoparietal preponder¬
ance of cortical atrophy in Fried¬
however, found cortical atrophy in
a small proportion of patients
It seems possible that the patients
were impaired in evaluating the test
materials because of their mild oculo¬
motor deficit. This argument would
conflict with the patients' normal per¬
formance on the progressive matrices.
Our clinical experience with the neu¬
bances would also not support this
Because there are no data available
on cognitive deficits in cerebellar dis¬
eases, it cannot be excluded that the
cerebellum might participate in cer¬
tain higher mental functions, namely,
in those that require operations in
space. The cerebellum is linked with
structures involved in cognitive func¬
tions by the corticopontocerebellar
pathway and the uncinate tract, and
the outflow tracts from the cerebellar
nuclei to the vestibular nuclei, reticu¬
lar formation, and ventral lateral and
ventral anterior nuclei of the thala¬
mus. Additional studies of the effects
of cerebellar damage on higher men¬
Since Friedreich's ataxia already
leads to severe impairments in adoles¬
cence, most of the patients in this
motor handicaps and somatosensory
deficits. Thus, deficits of construction
in space, even if it does not require
movements, might be due to a lack of
training. This argument seems to be
plausible for the deficits in the mental
folding and in the block design. The
arrangement might, of course, result
from a parallel deficit of visual per¬
ception. This interpretation, however,
seems rather unlikely. There is too
little known about which functions
are involved in the picture arrange¬
ment28 to interpret this result on the
In summary, we found no evidence
dementia. A deficit in tasks of spatial
construction can be explained either
by lack of training because of the
patient's long history of severe motor
and somatosensory handicaps or by a
cerebellar participation in those cog¬
nitive functions that rely on spatial
analysis and operations. A deficit in a
sequential organization (picture
rangement) was found, but requires
confirmation and further analysis for
This investigation was supported by grant Br
schaft. The studies are part of the medical thesis
of Ms Fehrenbach, who was supported by the
Studienstiftung des Deutschen Volkes.
